title: Forking Workflow
content:
### Fork vs Branch Workflow

| Branch Workflow | Fork Workflow |
|---|---|
| Contributors have write access | Contributors fork the repo |
| Branches in the same repo | Changes in personal fork |
| Used in teams | Used in open source |
| Simple setup | More steps but more control |

### Forking for Open Source

1. Fork the repository on GitHub (click "Fork")
2. Clone your fork locally:

```bash
git clone https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/repo.git
```

3. Add the original repo as an upstream remote:

```bash
git remote add upstream https://github.com/original-owner/repo.git
git remote -v
```

### Syncing a Fork with Upstream

Keep your fork up to date with the original repo:

```bash
git fetch upstream
git switch main
git merge upstream/main
git push origin main
```

### Contributing

1. Create a feature branch in your fork
2. Make changes and commit
3. Push to your fork: `git push -u origin feature`
4. Open a PR from your fork's branch to the upstream repo

### Fork Etiquette

- Keep your fork synced before starting new work
- Use descriptive branch names
- Squash commits before PR if requested
- Be responsive to reviewer feedback
- Respect the project's contributing guidelines
codeExample:
# Clone your fork
git clone https://github.com/myuser/awesome-project.git
cd awesome-project
git remote add upstream https://github.com/original/awesome-project.git

# Sync your fork
git fetch upstream
git checkout main
git merge upstream/main
git push origin main

quiz.question: In a fork workflow, what is the conventional name for the original repository remote?
What is the first step to sync a fork with the upstream repository?
